front: Building: Protestant church in Ibaraki, Osaka — cruciform slit cut into the east concrete wall is the only light source, casting a cross over the altar
Building: concrete walls cut at a 15-degree angle; benches and floor made from repurposed scaffolding planks; no conventional doors or windows
Building: open-air passageway leads into an interior blinded by light flooding through two intersecting gaps in the far wall

Building: chapel in Hokkaido attached to the Alpha Resort Hotel — employs 'borrowed scenery'; glass-and-steel cube intersects a concrete prism; floor-to-ceiling window aligned with a reflecting pool bearing a cross
Building: 1976 row house in Osaka (Azuma House) — completely blank prefabricated concrete facade with no exterior windows; interior organized around a narrow courtyard open to the sky
Building: almost entirely subterranean art museum on Naoshima island — natural light enters through concrete-framed geometric skylights; houses works by Monet, James Turrell, and Walter De Maria
Architect: self-taught Japanese architect born 1941 in Osaka; won the 1995 Pritzker Prize; signature approach: exposed reinforced concrete used to manipulate natural light
Architect: Hill of the Buddha (Hokkaido) — artificial hill planted with lavender surrounding a colossal Buddha statue
Architect: restored Punta della Dogana in Venice — triangular art museum between two canals, topped by a sculpture of two slaves holding a golden ball with Fortune on a wind vane
Architect: 21_21 Design Sight Tokyo — trapezoidal hand-sanded steel roof; Wood Culture Museum in Kansai — truncated conical form
Architect: Benesse Art House Project on Naoshima contains James Turrell's Back Side of the Moon; uses yakisugi / charred wood on the exterior
Architect: Pulitzer Arts Foundation in St. Louis — a Richard Serra weathering steel spiral stands in the courtyard; designed mansions for Beyonce ($200 million Malibu) and Kanye West
